explanations.8: This creates a reference to the array passed as the first argument — think of it as an alias so we can access the original array's contents inside the function.
explanations.14: This checks if the 'header' key exists in the array; if it doesn't exist, it sets header to an empty string, otherwise it uses the value from the array.
explanations.15: Same pattern as line 14, but for the 'method' key with a default value of 'GET' instead of empty.
explanations.16: Same pattern as line 14, but for the 'payload' key.
explanations.17: Same pattern as line 14, but for the 'proxy' key.
explanations.18: Same pattern as line 14, but for the 'token' key.
explanations.31: The eval keyword executes the string stored in cmd as if you had typed it directly — this is powerful but risky because it can run unexpected code if the string contains special characters.
explanations.33: The $? variable holds the exit status (success or failure code) of the last command, so this checks if the curl command failed.
